content:
# Luke Chapter 21 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א, 01), Quire 74 (marked ΛΓΥ/ΛΥΚΗ), PDF pages 114–115 | **Source Images:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_021_page_001.jpg, LUK_021_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And having-looked-up he saw the [ones] casting their gifts into the treasury — the rich [ones].

**2** And he saw a certain widow, a poor-one, casting there two lepta [lit. thin-coins],

**3** and he said, "Truly I say to you that this widow, the poor-one, more than all cast [in];

**4** for all these out of their surplus cast [in] into the gifts, but this [woman] out of her lack — all the livelihood which she was-having — she cast [in]."

**5** And [while] some were-speaking concerning the temple, that with beautiful stones and dedicated-offerings it has-been-adorned, he said,

**6** "These [things] which you are-beholding — days will-come in which a stone upon a stone will not be-left here which will not be-thrown-down."

**7** And they questioned him, saying, "Teacher, when therefore will these [things] be? And what [is] the sign when these [things] are-about to happen?"

**8** And he said, "See [that] you are not led-astray. For many will-come upon my name, saying, 'I am [he],' and 'The time has-drawn-near.' Do not go after them.

**9** And when you should-hear [of] wars and disorders, do not be-terrified; for it is-necessary [for] these [things] to happen first, but the end [is] not immediately."

**10** Then he was-saying to them, "Nation will-be-raised against nation, and kingdom against kingdom;

**11** and great earthquakes, and throughout [various] places pestilences and famines will-be, and fearful-things and great signs from heaven will-be.

**12** But before all these [things] they will-lay their hands upon you and will-persecute [you], delivering [you] over to the synagogues and prisons, being-led-away before kings and governors on-account-of my name.

**13** It will-result for you into a testimony.

**14** Place [it] therefore in your hearts not to practice-beforehand to make-a-defense;

**15** for I will-give to you a mouth and wisdom which all the [ones] opposing you will not be-able to withstand or to speak-against.

**16** And you will-be-delivered-over even by parents and brothers and relatives and friends, and they will-put-to-death [some] from among you,

**17** and you will-be being-hated by all on-account-of my name.

**18** And a hair from your head will by-no-means perish.

**19** In your endurance you will-acquire your souls.

**20** And when you should-see Jerusalem being-surrounded by encampments, then know that her desolation has-drawn-near.

**21** Then let the [ones] in Judea flee into the mountains, and let the [ones] in her midst depart-out, and let not the [ones] in the regions enter into her;

**22** because these are days of vengeance, [for] all the [things] having-been-written to be-fulfilled.

**23** Woe to the [ones] having [child] in womb and to the [ones] nursing in those days; for there will-be great distress upon the land and wrath to this people,

**24** and they will-fall by [the] mouth of [the] sword and will-be-led-captive into all the nations, and Jerusalem will-be being-trampled by [the] nations, until [the] times of [the] nations should-be-fulfilled.

**25** And there will-be signs in sun and moon and stars, and upon the earth distress of nations in perplexity [at the] sound of [the] sea and surging,

**26** [with] men fainting from fear and expectation of the [things] coming-upon the inhabited-world, for the powers of the heavens will-be-shaken.

**27** And then they will-see the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and much glory.

**28** And [when] these [things] begin to happen, stand-up-straight and lift-up your heads, because your redemption draws-near."

**29** And he spoke a parable to them: "See the fig-tree and all the trees:

**30** when they already put-forth [leaves], seeing [it] for yourselves you know that already the summer is near.

**31** So also you, when you should-see these [things] happening, know that the kingdom of God is near.

**32** Truly I say to you that this generation will by-no-means pass-away until all [things] should-happen.

**33** The heaven and the earth will-pass-away, but my words will by-no-means pass-away.

**34** And take-heed to yourselves, lest your hearts should-be-weighed-down in dissipation and drunkenness and anxieties of life, and that day should-come-upon you suddenly,

**35** as a snare; for it will-come-upon all the [ones] sitting upon [the] face of all the earth.

**36** And be-watchful in every season, praying that you may-have-strength to escape all these [things] — the [things] being-about to happen — and to stand before the Son of Man."

**37** And he was during the days teaching in the temple, and during the nights going-out he was-lodging at the mountain called [the mount] of Olives.

**38** And all the people were-rising-early [to come] to him in the temple to hear him.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **v.1 — Word order:** Sinaiticus reads αναβλεψας δε ειδεν τους βαλλοντας εις το γαζοφυλακιον τα δωρα αυτων πλουσιους — placing "rich [ones]" (πλουσιους) at the end of the clause as a kind of appositional afterthought. This word order is preserved in the translation with the em-dash construction. The verb αναβλεπω ("to look-up") suggests Jesus raised his eyes from a lower position, perhaps seated as a teacher in the temple courts.

2. **v.2 — λεπτα δυο (lepta duo):** Sinaiticus reads "two lepta" — the smallest copper coin in circulation, worth 1/128 of a denarius. Mark 12:42 glosses these as a kodrantēs (quadrans). Sinaiticus preserves the unglossed Lukan form. The word πενιχρα ("poor/needy") is stronger than πτωχος (v.3); it denotes someone at the edge of subsistence — this word appears only here in the NT.

3. **v.4 — εβαλεν (ebalen):** Aorist indicative, "cast [in]." Sinaiticus uses the simple aorist throughout vv.1–4 for the widow's action. The word βιος means "livelihood/means-of-living" (not "life" in the biological sense — that would be ζωη). The imperfect ειχεν ("was-having") suggests ongoing means of sustenance, not a windfall.

4. **v.5 — αναθημασιν (anathēmasin):** "Dedicated-offerings/votive-gifts" — not to be confused with αναθεμα ("accursed thing"), though both derive from ανατιθημι ("to set up"). The perfect passive κεκοσμηται ("has been adorned") indicates an accomplished, visible state. Josephus describes the temple's white marble overlaid with gold (War 5.222-224).

5. **v.8 — εγω ειμι (egō eimi):** Sinaiticus preserves the bare "I am" without predicate complement — rendered "I am [he]" with the implied predicate bracketed. The phrase echoes the divine self-identification (Exodus 3:14; Isaiah 43:10). The word καιρος ("the decisive, appointed time") is used rather than χρονος ("chronological time").

6. **v.9 — ακαταστασιας (akatastasias):** "Disorders/instabilities/revolutions" — from α-privative + καταστασις ("settled condition"). This word appears in Luke only here. The verb πτοεω ("to terrify") occurs only twice in the NT (here and Luke 24:37). The phrase αλλ ουκ ευθεως το τελος ("but the end [is] not immediately") is Luke's distinctive addition, tempering apocalyptic urgency.

7. **v.11 — λοιμοι και λιμοι (loimoi kai limoi):** Sinaiticus reads "pestilences and famines" — the near-homophonic pairing creates a deliberate wordplay. Some manuscripts reverse the order or omit one term. The word φοβητρον ("fearful-thing/terror") is a NT hapax legomenon.

8. **v.12 — προ δε τουτων παντων:** "But before all these [things]" — a crucial temporal marker unique to Luke: persecution precedes the cosmic signs. The verb παραδιδωμι ("to hand-over/deliver-up") is the same verb used for Judas' betrayal. The dual settings — synagogues (Jewish courts) and prisons (Roman system) — anticipate the double jurisdiction narrated in Acts.

9. **v.14 — προμελεταν (promeletan):** "To practice-beforehand" — a NT hapax legomenon. The compound verb προ-μελεταω combines "before" + "to practice/rehearse," a term from rhetoric for preparing a speech in advance. The promise in v.15 — "a mouth and wisdom" — pairs eloquence (form) with content (substance), both divinely supplied.

10. **v.16 — θανατωσουσιν εξ υμων:** "They will-put-to-death [some] from among you" — the partitive εξ indicates not all will die, but some. The paradox with v.18 (not a hair will perish) is deliberate: physical death is real, but ultimate destruction is impossible for those who belong to Christ.

11. **v.19 — κτησεσθε (ktēsesthe):** Sinaiticus reads the future middle indicative "you will-acquire" — a promise rather than a command. Some witnesses read the aorist imperative κτησασθε ("acquire!"). The word υπομονη ("endurance/patient-steadfastness," from υπο "under" + μενω "to remain") denotes active, resolute persistence under pressure.

12. **v.20 — στρατοπεδων (stratopedōn):** "Encampments/armies" — from στρατος ("army") + πεδον ("ground"). Where Mark 13:14 and Matthew 24:15 use the apocalyptic "abomination of desolation," Luke translates into concrete military language. The passive participle κυκλουμενην ("being-surrounded") describes a siege — precisely what Titus carried out in AD 70.

13. **v.22 — ημεραι εκδικησεως:** "Days of vengeance" — εκδικησις carries the sense of divine justice executed through historical events. The phrase του πλησθηναι παντα τα γεγραμμενα grounds the destruction in prophetic Scripture (cf. Deuteronomy 32:35; Hosea 9:7; Jeremiah 5:29).

14. **v.24 — αιχμαλωτισθησονται (aichmalōtisthēsontai):** Future passive, "will-be-led-captive" — from αιχμη ("spear") + αλωτος ("captured"). Josephus records 97,000 Jews taken captive during Jerusalem's fall (War 6.420). The phrase στοματι μαχαιρης ("by [the] mouth of [the] sword") is a Semitism (Hebrew: לְפִי־חֶרֶב). The periphrastic εσται πατουμενη ("will-be being-trampled") indicates ongoing, continuous action. The phrase καιροι εθνων ("times of the nations") is unique to Luke and implies a divinely appointed period of Gentile dominion.

15. **v.25 — ηχους...σαλου:** "Sound/roaring...surging" — σαλος ("surging/tossing") is a NT hapax describing storm-tossed sea. The word συνοχη ("distress," from συνεχω "to press-together") denotes being hemmed in. The word απορια ("perplexity," from α-privative + πορος "way") means literally "without-a-way-through."

16. **v.26 — αποψυχοντων (apopsychontōn):** Genitive absolute, "men fainting" — literally "breathing-away [one's life]" from terror. A NT hapax. The word οικουμενη ("inhabited-world") is the technical term for the civilized world. The δυναμεις ("powers") of the heavens being shaken echoes Isaiah 34:4 and Joel 2:10.

17. **v.27 — νεφελη (nephelē), singular:** Sinaiticus reads "in a cloud" (singular), not "in clouds" (plural, as in Mark 13:26). The singular may evoke the Shekinah cloud of God's presence (Exodus 40:34; 1 Kings 8:10-11). Direct allusion to Daniel 7:13-14.

18. **v.28 — ανακυψατε (anakupsate):** "Stand-up-straight" — literally "to unbend," as one stooped under a burden straightens up. The word απολυτρωσις ("redemption," from απο "from" + λυτρον "ransom-price") denotes liberation by payment of ransom. While the nations faint from fear (v.26), disciples are to stand tall.

19. **v.29 — και παντα τα δενδρα:** Luke adds "and all the trees" to Mark's fig-tree-only parable — broadening for a Gentile audience. The verb προβαλλω ("to put-forth/sprout," from προ "forward" + βαλλω "to throw") describes leaves pushing from buds.

20. **v.32 — η γενεα αυτη (hē genea hautē):** "This generation" — the most natural reading is the contemporaries of Jesus (~30-40 years), fulfilled in the destruction of Jerusalem (AD 70). The double negative ου μη with aorist subjunctive is the strongest possible Greek negation.

21. **v.33 — ου μη παρελθωσιν (ou mē parelthōsin):** Sinaiticus uses the subjunctive with ου μη for emphatic future negation in both clauses. Jesus places his own words above the cosmic order in permanence — a claim to divine authority (cf. Isaiah 40:8).

22. **v.34 — κραιπαλη (kraipalē):** "Dissipation" — literally the nausea/stupor following excessive drinking. A NT hapax. Paired with μεθη ("drunkenness") it creates a progression: drunkenness leads to morning-after stupor. The word μεριμναι βιωτικαι ("anxieties of life") extends the warning beyond debauchery to ordinary preoccupation.

23. **v.35 — ως παγις (hōs pagis):** "As a snare" — the image of a bird-trap springing shut. Some Western witnesses omit vv.34b–36 or rearrange; Sinaiticus preserves the full text. The adjective αιφνιδιος ("sudden") appears only here and 1 Thessalonians 5:3 in the NT.

24. **v.36 — αγρυπνειτε (agrypneite):** "Be-watchful" — present imperative, continuous action. From α-privative + υπνος ("sleep") via αγρευω ("to be in the field") — literally "to keep-watch-in-the-field." Sinaiticus reads this rather than the variant γρηγορειτε found in some manuscripts. The verb κατισχυω ("to have-strength/prevail") means to overpower, to be strong enough. The final infinitive σταθηναι ("to stand") is aorist passive — to take one's stand confidently before the returning Son of Man.

25. **v.37 — ηυλιζετο (ēulizeto):** Imperfect middle, "was-lodging" — from αυλη ("courtyard/open-air enclosure"), originally meaning to sleep in the open. The customary imperfect indicates habitual action during the Passion week. Jesus lodged on the Mount of Olives — from which, according to Zechariah 14:4, YHWH will stand when he comes to deliver Jerusalem.

26. **v.38 — ωρθριζεν (ōrthrizen):** Imperfect, "were-rising-early" — from ορθρος ("dawn"). A NT hapax appearing only here. The imperfect again indicates habitual action: the people came at first light daily to hear Jesus.

### Sigla
- **א** = Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE)
- Brackets **[ ]** indicate words implied by the Greek but not explicitly present in the source text
- Hyphens in English compounds preserve single Greek words that require multiple English words
- Parenthetical transliterations follow standard scholarly convention

---
*Translated and annotated by EMET, reading directly from Codex Sinaiticus manuscript images (Quire 74, folios corresponding to PDF pages 114–115). Fidelity to the source text is the governing principle — truth before beauty.*
